description: Show what the engine is building — what's next, what needs your review, and known issues — at a glance on a GitHub Projects board. One-time setup creates a board on your GitHub account and asks for a permission that covers all your GitHub Projects (optional and revocable), plus an optional one-time click in GitHub to turn on auto-add. A board is never required: the engine works the same from your issues and pull requests, and you can delete the board later without losing anything.

Before a change you've asked for gets built, a fresh set of reviewers checks the plan first — whether it solves the right problem, whether the design is sound, whether it can actually be built and run, and whether it could be unsafe or break a rule it must follow. They only advise; you decide, and you merge.

After a change you've asked for is built — before it's submitted — a fresh set of reviewers checks the finished work: whether it does what was asked, whether it's pleasant to use, whether it's soundly built, and whether it's safe to release. They only advise; you decide, and you merge.

A standing rule for your project's database migrations — the scripts that change your database's shape or move its data. Before a change that could lose data or can't be undone, the engine is set to stop and bring you in — pausing to ask when you're working with it, and holding the work for you to see when it's running unattended — in plain language and with a safer option, instead of pressing ahead on its own. It's a habit the engine follows, backed by the pull request you review and approve, not an automatic scanner — and it never runs a migration for you. Optional, and removable anytime.

This add-on adds an automatic check on the outside code your project depends on — the third-party libraries it pulls in — that can block the merge when a change brings in a risky one. When a dependency is added or updated, the check reviews it and stops the change from going in if the library has a known security hole, or comes with a license that could force you to publish your own source code in return. It also gives gentler, non-blocking nudges to use exact versions of your libraries instead of open-ended ranges. Nothing is ever a dead end: a genuinely unavoidable case can be let through with a deliberate, recorded decision, and you can leave this add-on out altogether — either way, nothing merges without your say-so.

Lets the engine offer your changes to a project you don't own — an open-source project you've forked, or any upstream you contribute to — as a pull request from your fork, carrying only your project's files and never the engine's own. It does the mechanical git a non-engineer shouldn't have to, and opening the pull request is always your call. Two things to weigh before you opt in. First, whether a change is accepted is the upstream project's decision — not yours, and not the engine's. Second, this cross-project path has not yet been run from end to end against a live project: the final step that opens the real pull request has never run for real, so your first contribution would be the first time it runs anywhere — the rest is built and checked, but you would be the one proving that last step in practice. Once it's added, you use it by asking the engine to offer a change upstream; there's no command to remember. You can leave this add-on out, and nothing else changes if you do.
